claim_text: The combination of physical examinations with C-reactive protein values do not improve the accuracy of radiographic verified pneumonia predictions.

runs.response_text:
VERDICT: CONTRADICT

The first abstract directly addresses this claim and provides evidence that contradicts it. The study found that adding C-reactive protein (CRP) at a cut-off of >30 mg/L to clinical symptoms and signs improved diagnostic accuracy for pneumonia, increasing the ROC area from 0.70 to 0.77 and providing a net reclassification improvement of 28%. The authors explicitly concluded that "Addition of CRP concentration at the optimal cut off of >30 mg/L improved diagnostic information

VERDICT: CONTRADICT  
The first abstract provides evidence that the addition of C-reactive protein (CRP) values improves the diagnostic accuracy for predicting pneumonia, as indicated by an increase in the ROC area when CRP is included in the assessment. This directly contradicts the claim.

VERDICT: CONTRADICT
The abstract states that the addition of C-reactive protein (CRP) concentration to symptoms and signs improved the diagnostic accuracy for predicting pneumonia, with an increased area under the receiver operating characteristic curve.

VERDICT: CONTRADICT

The first abstract (doc_id 5558754) directly contradicts this claim, showing that adding CRP at a cut-off of >30 mg/L to symptoms and signs increased the ROC area from 0.70 to 0.77 and improved diagnostic classification with a net reclassification improvement of 28% for radiographically verified pneumonia.

VERDICT: CONTRADICT
Abstract [doc_id 5558754] states that the addition of CRP at the optimal cut-off of >30 mg/L increased the ROC area to 0.77 from 0.70 of the clinical prediction items alone, and improved the diagnostic classification, directly contradicting the claim.
